First-Degree Intentional Homicide—Causing the death of another human being with the specific intent to kill that person or another.
Attempted First-Degree Intentional Homicide—An act demonstrating unequivocally that the actor intended to and would have caused the death of another but for the intervention of an extraneous factor.
First-Degree Intentional Homicide as a Persistent Offender—Intentionally causing death after having been previously convicted of two or more serious violent felonies.
Murder in the First Degree (Mitigated Tier)—Causing death with intent to kill under mitigating circumstances (Adequate Provocation, Unnecessary Defensive Force, Coercion, or Necessity).

Murder with Special Circumstances (Aggravated Intentional Homicide)—Intentional homicide involving aggravating factors (e.g., law enforcement victim, mass casualty, torture) resulting in a non-release mandate.
First-Degree Reckless Homicide—Recklessly causing the death of another human being under circumstances which show utter disregard for human life.
Second-Degree Reckless Homicide—Recklessly causing the death of another human being without the elevated element of utter disregard for human life.
Second-Degree Reckless Homicide of an Unborn Child—Recklessly causing the death of an unborn child by creating an unreasonable risk of death or great bodily harm.

Second-Degree Intentional Homicide (Voluntary Manslaughter Equivalent)—Causing the death of another person with the specific intent to kill, but where the offense is mitigated under § 940.01(2) (Adequate Provocation, Coercion, Necessity, or Unnecessary Defensive Force).

Second-Degree Intentional Homicide (Adequate Provocation / Heat of Passion Branch)—Intentional killing where the defendant's intent was strictly caused by adequate provocation that would deprive a reasonable person of self-control.

Homicide by Negligent Handling of Dangerous Weapon, Explosives or Fire—Causing the death of another person by the high degree of negligence in the operation or handling of a dangerous weapon, explosives, or fire.

Homicide by Negligent Handling of Dangerous Weapon (Unborn Child)—Causing the death of an unborn child by the high degree of negligence in the operation or handling of a dangerous weapon, explosives, or fire.
Homicide by Negligent Operation of a Vehicle—Causing the death of another human being by the criminally negligent operation or handling of a vehicle.
Homicide by Negligent Operation of a Vehicle (Unborn Child)—Causing the death of an unborn child by the negligent operation or handling of a vehicle.
Homicide by Intoxicated Use of a Vehicle—Causing the death of another by the operation or handling of a vehicle while under the influence of an intoxicant.

Homicide by Intoxicated Use of a Vehicle (PAC)—Causing the death of another by the operation or handling of a vehicle while the person has a prohibited alcohol concentration as defined under § 340.01(46m).

Homicide by Intoxicated Use (Restricted Controlled Substance)—Causing the death of another person by operating a vehicle with any detectable amount of a restricted controlled substance (e.g., Delta-9-THC, Methamphetamine, Cocaine) in the blood.
Homicide by Intoxicated Use of a Vehicle (Unborn Child)—Causing the death of an unborn child by the operation or handling of a vehicle while under the influence of an intoxicant.

Homicide by Intoxicated Use of a Vehicle (With Aggravating Prior)—Causing the death of another by intoxicated operation of a vehicle when the defendant has a prior conviction or suspension history under OWI guidelines.

Homicide by Intoxicated Use of a Vehicle with Prior OWI (Involuntary Manslaughter Aggravated Tier)—Causing death through impaired operation when the defendant carries a qualified prior driving enhancement.

Homicide by Intoxicated Use of a Commercial Motor Vehicle—Causing the death of another person by operating a commercial motor vehicle while under the influence of an intoxicant or with a blood alcohol concentration of 0.04 or more.

Fleeing or Eluding an Officer Resulting in Death—Knowingly operating a motor vehicle to flee or attempt to elude a law enforcement officer after receiving a visual or audible signal, resulting in the death of another person.

Hit and Run Involving Death—Failure of a vehicle operator involved in an accident resulting in injury or death to immediately stop, remain at the scene, fulfill identification duties, and render reasonable assistance, where the accident results in the death of any person.

Assisting Suicide—With intent that another person commit suicide, intentionally providing the physical means, committing a physical act, or providing specialized assistance by which the other person commits or attempts to commit suicide.

First-Degree Reckless Homicide (Homicide by Child Abuse Branch)—Recklessly causing the death of a child under the age of 18 under circumstances showing an utter disregard for human life, while committing physical abuse of a child under § 948.03.

Attempted First-Degree Intentional Homicide—Acts demonstrating unequivocally that the actor intended to commit first-degree intentional homicide and would have done so except for the intervention of another person or an extraneous factor.

Felony Murder (Third-Degree Murder Equivalent)—Causing the death of another person while committing, or attempting to commit, specific unlisted underlying felonies: § 940.19/940.195 (Battery), § 940.225(1) to (3) (Sexual Assault), § 940.30 (False Imprisonment), § 940.31 (Kidnapping), § 943.02 (Arson), § 943.10 (Burglary), § 943.23(1g) (Carjacking), or § 943.32 (Robbery).

Homicide by Intoxicated Use of a Firearm—Causing the death of another person by the operation or handling of a firearm while under the influence of an intoxicant or with a prohibited blood alcohol concentration.

Homicide by Vicious Animal—An owner of a vicious animal, knowing its propensities, who intentionally allows it to go at large or keeps it without ordinary care, resulting in the animal killing a human being who took all reasonable precautions.

Felony Murder (Party to a Crime Modifier)—Liability for a homicide committed by an accomplice during the execution of a specified predicate felony, where the defendant intentionally aided, abetted, or conspired in the underlying crime.

Active Arrest Warrant (Felony Homicide Nexus)—A judicially authorized order commanding law enforcement to arrest a named individual based on a showing of probable cause for a homicide or related felony offense.

Arrest Record for Serious Felony / Homicide Nexus—The physical taking of a person into custody by a law enforcement officer based on probable cause, generating a permanent state tracking entry prior to formal court adjudication.

Refusal to Submit to Chemical Testing (Implied Consent Violation)—An administrative and civil infraction occurring when a driver refuses an officer's lawful request for a breath, blood, or urine sample under a vehicular homicide or OWI investigation.

Reckless Driving (Municipal Adoption)—Operating a motor vehicle on a highway in a manner that endangers the safety of any person or property. Charged locally when death/injury elements are unproven or processed as a parallel local infraction.

Operating While Intoxicated (Municipal 1st Offense)—Operating a motor vehicle under the influence of an intoxicant, a controlled substance, or with a prohibited alcohol concentration of 0.08 or more within local boundaries.
Operating While Intoxicated (Municipal 1st Offense)—Operating a motor vehicle under the influence of an intoxicant or with a blood/breath alcohol concentration of 0.08 or more within local borders.
Reckless Driving (Municipal Baseline)—Operating a motor vehicle on a highway in a manner that endangers the safety of any person or property.

Disorderly Conduct (Municipal)—Engaging in violent, abusive, indecent, profane, boisterous, unreasonably loud or otherwise disorderly conduct under circumstances in which the conduct tends to cause or provoke a disturbance.

Discharge of Firearms within City Borders—Firing or discharging any gun, pistol, or firearm within the city limits (except in licensed shooting galleries, by law enforcement officers, or in lawful self-defense).
Arson of Buildings—By means of fire or explosives, intentionally damaging or destroying any building of another without their consent, or any building with intent to defraud an insurer.
Arson of Property Other Than Buildings—Intentionally damaging or destroying property of another (excluding buildings) by means of fire or explosives, where the property value exceeds $100.
Arson with Intent to Defraud—Setting fire to or burning any property with intent to defraud an insurer, regardless of whether the property belongs to the actor or another.

Criminal Disorderly Conduct—Engaging in violent, abusive, indecent, profane, boisterous, unreasonably loud, or otherwise disorderly conduct in public or private under circumstances in which the conduct tends to cause or provoke a disturbance.
Commercial Gambling—Intentionally operating or permitting an unauthorized commercial lottery, booking bets, or maintaining a gambling place for profit.
Unpermitted Local Gambling—Organizing, participating in, or permitting unpermitted local bingo, raffles, lotteries, or low-tier unauthorized betting structures inside municipal borders.
Solicitation of Children to Join a Criminal Gang—Intentionally soliciting, enticing, or intimidating a child under the age of 18 to participate in or join a criminal gang organization.
Prostitution—Intentionally engaging in, or offering or agreeing to engage in, sexual activity or contact with another person for anything of value.
Prostitution / Solicitation (Municipal Tier)—Engaging in or offering to engage in prostitution or public solicitation within city limits, processed as an ordinance infraction.

Inciting or Participating in a Riot—Intentionally failing to withdraw from an unlawful assembly after being lawfully commanded to disperse by law enforcement officers, thereby participating in a riot.
Criminal Trespass to Dwellings—Intentionally entering or remaining in the dwelling of another person without the consent of a person lawfully entitled to possession.
Trespass to Land (Municipal)—Entering or remaining on the open land, commercial lot, or physical property of another without lawful authority or consent of the owner or occupant.
Criminal Damage to Property (Baseline)—Intentionally causing damage to any physical property of another person without that person's consent, where the reduction in value does not exceed $2,500.

Aggravated Criminal Damage to Property—Intentionally causing damage to the physical property of another without consent, where property value reduction exceeds $2,500, or where property belongs to public utilities, state networks, or specific protected environments.

Permitting Use of Gambling Device / Asset Forfeiture—The manufacturing, selling, renting, or permitting of an unauthorized gambling device on a business premises, triggering automated civil property seizure.
Aggravated Gang Intimidation—Utilizing force, violence, or direct threats of bodily harm to compel any individual to participate in, join, or remain a member of a criminal gang organization.

Criminal Gang Penalty Enhancement—A statutory enhancer applied to any misdemeanor or felony conviction where the underlying crime was explicitly committed for the benefit of, at the direction of, or in association with a criminal gang.

Prostitution Nuisance Vehicle Impoundment—The administrative seizure and physical impoundment of a motor vehicle used to facilitate or solicit prostitution within designated nuisance enforcement zones.

Commercial Gambling Device Forfeiture—The administrative seizure, civil confiscation, and court-ordered destruction of illegal gambling devices, machines, logs, and associated cash proceeds used in unauthorized operations.

Refusal to Disperse / Unlawful Assembly—Failing or refusing to intentionally withdraw or disperse from a public location after receiving a lawful command to clear the street/area from law enforcement.

Criminal Trespass to a Medical Facility—Intentionally entering or remaining in a medical facility (hospitals, clinics, or sanatoriums) without the consent of a person lawfully authorized to give consent.

Warrant for Failure to Appear (Trespass Context)—A judicially issued bench warrant commanding law enforcement to arrest an individual for failing to appear in court after being lawfully cited for a criminal or municipal trespass infraction.
Attempted Riot / Inciting Riot—Acts demonstrating unequivocally an intent to organize, incite, or participate in a riot that are interrupted or fall short of completion.

Interference with Agricultural Inspectors—Intentionally obstructing, hindering, or refusing entry to an authorized DATCP inspector engaged in execution of food safety, crop health, or livestock monitoring duties.

Commercial Signal Piracy / Telecommunications Interception Devices—The manufacturing, distribution, sale, or lease of any equipment, decoder, or software designed to facilitate the unauthorized interception or decryption of commercial telecommunications, satellite, or cable networks for financial gain.
Plant Inspection Refusal—Refusing to permit a lawful agricultural inspection of crops, nurseries, or plant shipments by authorized DATCP personnel seeking to contain pest or disease outbreaks.

Malicious Destruction of Livestock Fences—Intentionally cutting, tearing down, or destroying any fence or property boundary enclosing livestock, creating a severe biosecurity hazard or property escape.

Criminal Damage to Property Involved in Commercial Livestock Operation—Intentionally causing damage to property, fences, facilities, or automated infrastructure utilized in a commercial livestock operation without consent.

Pesticide Law Violations / Fraudulent Application—Intentionally applying restricted-use pesticides without a valid license, falsifying application records, or deploying toxic chemicals in direct violation of state environmental safety controls.

Commercial Pesticide License Denial, Suspension, or Revocation—The structural administrative revocation of commercial chemical certifications due to fraudulent data recording, equipment misuse, or environmental negligence.

Illegal Tampering with Water Flow Gates / Irrigation Ditches—Intentionally altering, damming, or destroying any public or private irrigation gate, flow structure, or water channel to divert public waters without a permit or to damage agricultural property.

Transportation of Quarantined or Diseased Livestock—Knowingly moving, selling, or transporting animals that are infected with a contagious disease or originate from an active quarantine zone without a valid DATCP state permit.

Emergency Animal Quarantine Violation (Administrative)—Failing to comply with isolation orders, diagnostic testing directives, or movement restrictions issued by the state veterinarian during a livestock disease outbreak.

Weights and Measures Device Tampering—Intentionally altering, modifying, or manipulating a commercial weighing or measuring device (such as a gas pump, grocery scale, or grain elevator scale) to record an inaccurate metric for consumer trade.

Commercial Scale Seal Alteration (Administrative)—Removing, breaking, or altering an official lead seal or regulatory tag applied by a state weights and measures inspector to a condemned, inaccurate, or untested commercial scale.

Breaking Security Seal on Condemned Commercial Scale—Removing, altering, or breaking an official state security tag or lead wire seal placed on an inaccurate commercial measuring device by a weights inspector prior to official re-certification.

Unlawful Short-Weight Consumer Sale (Municipal)—Selling, offering, or exposing for sale a lesser quantity of a commodity than represented, processed locally as a non-criminal consumer protection ticket.

Selling Short-Weight Commodities (Municipal)—Selling, offering, or exposing for sale a commodity in a quantity less than that represented, or misrepresenting the weight or measure of a retail good within city limits.
Mistreating Animals (Baseline)—Intentionally or negligently treating any animal in a cruel manner, including withholding adequate food, water, or shelter, or causing unnecessary suffering.
Felony Animal Mistreatment—Intentionally violating § 951.02 resulting in the mutilation, disfigurement, torture, or death of an animal.

Animal Neglect / At-Large Violation (Municipal)—Permitting an animal to run at large or failing to provide basic humane shelter and clean maintenance conditions, processed as a non-criminal city ticket.

Hunting Big Game Out of Season—Intentionally hunting, taking, catching, or killing big game (such as deer, bear, or elk) during the closed season, or without a valid state-issued conservation license.

Theft of Utility Services (Baseline)—Intentionally diverting, siphoning, or connecting to electric, gas, water, or steam services of another without consent and with intent to deprive the utility of payment, where the value of services does not exceed $2,500.

Water and Irrigation Tampering—Intentionally altering, damaging, or obstructing any public irrigation ditch, navigable water flow gate, or stream bulkhead to divert water resource assets without permission.

Unauthorized Diversion of Public Waters—The administrative diversion of water from lakes or streams for agricultural irrigation or industrial use without a valid permit from the DNR, or exceeding permitted volumetric limits.
Aggravated Felony Animal Abuse—Intentionally treating an animal in a cruel manner resulting in the mutilation, disfigurement, or death of the animal, or intentionally torturing an animal.

Commercial Wildlife Trafficking / Aggravated Poaching—The unlawful sale, purchase, barter, or commercial trafficking of protected wild animals, or large-scale poaching where the statutory value of the wildlife taken exceeds $2,500.

Felony Utility Theft—Intentionally diverting, siphoning, or connecting to commercial electric, gas, water, or telecom services without consent, where the cumulative market value of the stolen service exceeds $2,500.

Telecommunications Service Theft—Intentionally obtaining or attempting to obtain telecommunications service of another without consent by use of a deceptive device, unauthorized connection, or fraudulent credit profile, with intent to avoid payment.

Intercepting Wire, Electronic, or Oral Communications—Intentionally intercepting, attempting to intercept, or procuring another person to intercept a wire, electronic, or oral communication without the consent of at least one party to the communication.

Unlawful Utility Siphoning / Scavenging (Municipal)—The unauthorized connection to, siphoning of, or opening of city-managed water hydrants, public recycling frameworks, or municipal electrical nodes for personal or temporary use without paying established city fees.

Negotiating Worthless Instrument (Bad Check)—Intentionally issuing or delivering a check or draft for the payment of money, knowing that it will not be paid by the depository, where the financial instrument value does not exceed $2,500.

Negotiating Worthless Instrument (Aggravated Tier)—Intentionally issuing or delivering a check or draft knowing it will not be honored, where the check value exceeds $2,500, or issuing multiple checks totaling over $2,500 within a 90-day period.

NMLS Material Non-Disclosure—Failing to disclose a material criminal history or regulatory disciplinary history on an application for a mortgage loan originator, broker, or lender license submitted through the NMLS.

Mortgage Loan Originator (MLO) Statutory Revocation—The mandatory revocation of an MLO license by the DFI due to the licensee being convicted of any felony within the past 7 years, or any felony involving fraud, dishonesty, breach of trust, or money laundering at any time.

Child Labor Law Violations—An employer or agent of an employer intentionally employing, permitting, or suffering any minor to work in violation of statutory age limits, permitted hourly windows, or hazardous environment bans under Chapter 103.
anchors.subcategory: Professional Licensing Discipline & Institutional Welfare Actions

anchors.phrase: Failure to Secure Workers' Compensation Insurance
anchors.subcategory: Professional Licensing Discipline & Institutional Welfare Actions

anchors.phrase: Wage Theft (Intentional Evasion of Required Wages)—An employer intentionally and with intent to defraud failing to pay wages due to employees, or utilizing specialized corporate accounting schemes to avoid baseline or minimum wage metrics when the asset threshold crosses felony allocations.
anchors.subcategory: Professional Licensing Discipline & Institutional Welfare Actions

anchors.phrase: Wage and Minimum Wage Infractions (Municipal/Contractor)—An approved municipal service contractor failing to pay local baseline living-wage adjustments or indexing metrics required under city service development agreements.
anchors.subcategory: Professional Licensing Discipline & Institutional Welfare Actions

anchors.phrase: Money Laundering (Concealing Criminal Proceeds)—Intentionally conducting financial transactions involving property or currency derived from racketeering activity with the intent to conceal or disguise the nature, location, source, or ownership of the proceeds.

Unauthorized Practice of Law—Every person who appears as agent, attorney, or counselor in any court of record in this state, or maintains a law office, or holds themselves out as an attorney without having been admitted as an attorney by the Supreme Court of Wisconsin.

Fraudulent Practices in Securities Sales (Criminal Tier)—Intentionally employing any device, scheme, or artifice to defraud, making material misrepresentations, or breaching fiduciary obligations in connection with the offer, sale, or purchase of securities in Wisconsin.

Securities Agent and Investment Adviser Disqualification—A formal administrative order issued by the Department of Financial Institutions (DFI) revoking, suspending, or conditioning a registration, or barring a person from securities trade, for material omissions, failure to comply, or statutory financial infractions.

Unauthorized Practice of Medicine—Practicing medicine or surgery, attempting to do so, or holding oneself out as authorized to practice medicine or surgery in Wisconsin without holding a valid license issued by the Medical Examining Board.

Unlicensed Dwelling Contracting—Engaging in the business of dwelling contracting or HVAC, electrical, or plumbing installation without holding a valid state building credential or master qualification card required under Chapter 101.

DSPS Contractor Cease and Desist / Stop-Work Order—A formal administrative or court-backed civil injunction ordering a contractor to immediately stop operations for bond failures, major building code infractions, or executing commercial contracting without verification fields.

Insurance Intermediary License Revocation—A formal administrative order issued by the Office of the Commissioner of Insurance (OCI) revoking or suspending an insurance intermediary/producer license for fraudulent insurance practices, material misrepresentation, or financial dishonesty.

Real Estate License Revocation—A formal administrative order issued by the Real Estate Examining Board (DSPS) revoking or suspending a real estate broker or salesperson license for substantial misrepresentation, trust account mismanagement, or dual agency fraud.

Unlicensed Local Peddling or Transient Sales—Engaging in the business of a transient merchant, peddler, or solicitor within city borders without first obtaining a valid city commercial license or vendor registration.

DWD Administrative Stop-Work Order—A mandatory operational shutdown order issued by the Department of Workforce Development against an employer for failing to secure workers' compensation insurance or failing to pay assessed non-compliance fines.
anchors.subcategory: Professional Licensing Discipline & Institutional Welfare Actions

anchors.phrase: Human Trafficking (Sexual Exploitation)—Knowingly recruiting, enticing, harboring, transporting, providing, or obtaining a person for commercial sex acts through force, fraud, or coercion.

Human Trafficking (Labor / Involuntary Servitude)—Knowingly recruiting, enticing, harboring, transporting, providing, or obtaining a person for forced labor or services through debt bondage, psychological coercion, or physical threats.
Attempted Human Trafficking—Performing acts demonstrating unequivocally an intent to execute labor or sex trafficking under § 940.302, which are prevented or fall short of completion.
General Court-Martial Conviction—Conviction by a state military tribunal for violent offenses, major property theft, or espionage executed by national guard personnel while in active state service.

Desertion, Mutiny, or Insubordination—Any member of the state military forces who deserts, attempts to desert, quits their unit with intent to avoid hazardous duty, or creates or participates in any mutiny or sedition.
Attempted Military Desertion—An act done with specific intent to commit desertion under the code, amounting to more than mere preparation and tending, even if frustrated, to effect its commission.

State Military Apprehension Order (Desertion Nexus)—A formally authorized order issued by competent military command commanding civil law enforcement officers to apprehend a member of the state military forces charged with desertion.

Probation Supervision—A sentencing alternative where the court places a convicted defendant under the management of the state DOC, tracking strict reporting mandates, curfew controls, mandatory drug sweeps, and geofence tracking perimeters.

Probation Revocation Proceeding—The administrative and judicial cancellation of an offender's probation status due to technical violations or new criminal activity, triggering execution of a stayed prison sentence or a new initial sentencing hearing.

Parole / Supervised Release Mechanism—The conditional release of an incarcerated offender from state prison to community supervision, applicable strictly to legacy offenses committed prior to December 31, 1999.

Parole Revocation Proceeding—The formal administrative cancellation of an offender's legacy parole status by an Administrative Law Judge (ALJ) following technical or substantive violations, forcing immediate re-incarceration in state prison.

Community Correction Center Transfer—The administrative placement or diversion of an inmate or high-risk supervisee into a state-operated or contracted residential community correction center (halfway house) as an alternative to prison or a pre-release transition node.

Good Time / Earned Release Credit Application—The statutory system governing sentence reduction tracking, split between legacy pre-1999 prison 'good time' and modern TIS 'Earned Release Program (ERP)' / 'Challenge Academy' completions.

Electronic Tracking System / GPS Monitoring—The mandatory or discretionary placement of a supervised individual on active global positioning system (GPS) tracking as an explicit condition of parole, extended supervision, or specialized post-release tracking.

Huber Law Work Release Privileges—Judicially authorized or Sheriff-approved permission allowing an inmate confined in a county jail or house of correction to leave the facility for necessary employment, schooling, medical care, or childcare.

Failure to Comply with Supervision (Absconding)—The intentional failure of a person on probation, parole, or extended supervision to maintain contact with their DOC agent, fleeing their documented residence, or violating core supervision conditions.

Supervision Revocation Proceeding—The hybrid administrative and civil-criminal process adjudicated by the Division of Hearings and Appeals to formally cancel probation, parole, or extended supervision due to technical or substantive violations.

Judicial Record Sealing—The statutory or court-ordered process restricting public access to specific historical court records on the open Wisconsin CCAP portal after verifying explicit compliance criteria or specialized safety windows.

Expungement of Criminal Record—The structural process where a court orders a person's record expunged upon successful completion of a sentence, restricting public access and removing the conviction from the electronic circuit court automation program (CCAP).

DNA Record Expungement—The formal administrative process to destroy a person's DNA specimen, profile, and data bank entries held by the DOJ following an acquittal, dismissal, or reversal of all underlying criminal charges.

Administrative Criminal History Record Challenge—The administrative process by which an individual challenges inaccurate, incomplete, or factually erroneous entries on their official Crime Information Bureau (CIB) fingerprint-backed rap sheet.

Statutory Protections for Sealed Records in Employment—The statutory provisions under the Wisconsin Fair Employment Act (WFEA) regulating how sealed or expunged records interact with employer background check evaluations.

Employer Regulatory Access to Non-Public Records—The statutory framework defining which specialized categories of employers (e.g., childcare, law enforcement, caregiving) maintain lawful access to review sealed or expunged histories under state background check rules.
Gubernatorial Pardon—The discretionary act of executive clemency by the Governor of Wisconsin that forgives a past criminal conviction and fully restores an individual's basic civil rights.

Restoration of Voting Franchise Following Felony—The automated process by which individuals convicted of a felony regain their right to register and vote in Wisconsin elections upon absolute discharge from their entire sentence.

Restoration of Firearm Possession Rights—The structural legal pathways (or absolute limitations) governing how an individual convicted of a felony or a qualified misdemeanor can regain lawful authority to possess a firearm in Wisconsin.

Certificate of Rehabilitation (Wisconsin Non-Existence status)—Wisconsin law does not recognize or issue judicial 'Certificates of Rehabilitation' or 'Certificates of Employability' to override statutory felony bars.

Desertion / Absent Without Leave (WCMJ)—Any member of the state military forces (Wisconsin National Guard) who, without authority, goes or remains absent from their unit with intent to remain away permanently, avoid hazardous duty, or shirk important service.

UCMJ Federal Court-Martial Record Integration—The administrative tracking protocol by which the Wisconsin DOJ Crime Information Bureau ingests, indexes, and merges federal UCMJ court-martial convictions into state background check screening feeds.

Parole / Extended Supervision Revocation Order—The formal administrative final decision issued by the DHA cancelling parole or extended supervision status, executing a mandatory return to a state prison facility for a designated re-confinement period.

Alternative-to-Revocation (ATR) Center Transfer—The administrative diversion and physical transfer of a non-compliant probationer or parolee to a locked or staff-secure community corrections center or halfway house as a structured alternative to formal revocation.

Escape from Huber Confinement (Work Release Violation)—The intentional failure of a county jail inmate to return to the facility at the conclusion of authorized work release hours, or traveling outside the designated workplace boundaries without permission.

Juvenile Court Record Confidentiality—The mandatory, automated closure and sealing of all records of a court exercising jurisdiction under the Juvenile Justice Code, prohibiting public inspection or CCAP indexing.

Department of Corrections Apprehension Request / Violation Warrant—An administrative warrant issued directly by a DOC supervisor authorizing law enforcement officers to arrest and detain a probationer or parolee for active supervision violations.

Arrest Record for Supervision Technical Violation (Probation Hold)—The physical booking and fingerprinting footprint logged when an individual is detained by police or agents solely on a DOC administrative hold, without any new independent criminal charges.

Substantiated Child Abuse or Neglect Administrative Finding—A formal administrative determination by a county human services agency or the state DCF validating, by a preponderance of the evidence following an investigation, that a person has committed child abuse or physical/emotional neglect.

Substantiated Abuse or Exploitation of a Vulnerable Adult—A formal administrative determination by DHS confirming that a caregiver has committed an act of physical abuse, neglect, or financial exploitation against an endangered adult.

Professional Misconduct Finding—A formal determination of a statutory rule, professional code, or ethical violation executed by a state professional licensing board inside the DSPS system following an evidentiary review.

Issuance of Final Disciplinary Order Following Board Hearing—The execution of an enforceable final administrative decree by a state licensing board ordering the suspension, limitation, reprimand, or permanent revocation of a professional license.

Medicaid Program Provider Exclusion Action—The administrative exclusion or termination of a healthcare provider from participation in the Wisconsin Medical Assistance (Medicaid) program due to billing fraud, kickbacks, or systematic program abuse.

Federal LEIE Healthcare Exclusion Integration—The administrative tracking node executing immediate state compliance and integration with the federal OIG's List of Excluded Individuals and Entities (LEIE) following high-tier medical fraud.

Operating a Motorboat While Intoxicated (BUI Baseline)—Engaging in the operation or handling of a motorboat or vessel on state public waters while under the influence of an intoxicant, a restricted controlled substance, or with a prohibited blood alcohol concentration of 0.08 or more.

Operating a Motorboat While Intoxicated (Recidivist Tier)—Operating a motorboat or vessel on state public waters while under the influence of an intoxicant, a restricted controlled substance, or with a prohibited BAC of 0.08 or more, carrying one prior qualified OWI or BUI conviction within a 7-year tracking window.

Aggravated Criminal BUI Causing Injury—Operating a motorboat or vessel on state public waters while under the influence of an intoxicant, a restricted controlled substance, or with a prohibited BAC of 0.08 or more, causing great bodily harm or injury to another person.

Refusal to Submit to Chemical Testing (Boating)—Refusing a law enforcement officer or DNR warden's lawful request for a breath, blood, or urine sample under a boating under the influence (BUI) or fatal vessel crash investigation.

Boating Under the Influence (Municipal Adoption)—Operating a motorboat, personal watercraft, or vessel on navigable waters within city/county boundaries while under the influence of an intoxicant or with a BAC of 0.08 or more.

Theft of Movable Property (Felony Baseline)—Intentionally taking, carrying away, using, transferring, concealing, or retaining possession of movable property of another without consent and with intent to deprive the owner permanently of possession, where the value of the property exceeds $2,500 but does not exceed $5,000.

High-Tier Felony Property Theft—Intentionally taking, carrying away, transferring, or concealing property of another without consent, valued above $5,000 up to and exceeding $100,000, to permanently deprive the owner.

Retail Theft (Misdemeanor Baseline)—Concealing or taking possession of merchant inventory, altering price tags, or transferring containers to deprive a merchant permanently of the full purchase value, where the merchandise value does not exceed $2,500.

Aggravated Felony Retail Theft—Concealing, taking, or altering price tags of merchant merchandise valued above $2,500, or acting in concert with a theft ring or using theft-detection shielding devices to bypass merchant security parameters.

Armed Burglary—Violating the perimeters of any building or dwelling without consent and with intent to steal or commit a felony, while armed with a dangerous weapon, or arming oneself with a dangerous weapon while inside the structure, or executing a physical battery on a lawful occupant.

Attempted Burglary to a Dwelling—Performing acts demonstrating unequivocally an intent to enter a building or dwelling without consent to commit a theft or felony, which are interrupted or fall short of entry.
Attempted Petty Theft—Acts demonstrating unequivocally an intent to steal property or conceal merchandise under $2,500 that are prevented or fall short of completion.
Petty Shoplifting (Municipal Forfeiture)—Concealing or taking retail merchandise without consent inside city borders where the value matches low-tier local thresholds.

Retail Theft Civil Liability Demand—The statutory mechanism enabling retail merchants to sue or demand civil exemplary damages and retail restitution directly from any adult or emancipated minor who commits retail theft under § 943.50.

Property Restitution Judgment Lien—The conversion of a criminal court restitution order into an enforceable civil judgment lien against all real property owned by the defendant in the county of docketing, executing financial holds on property transfers.

Attempted Identity Theft—Performing acts demonstrating unequivocally an intent to use or possess another's personal identifying information without consent to obtain credit, money, or services, which are prevented or fall short of completion.

Bribery of Public Officers or Employees—Transferring, offering, or promising to a public officer or public employee any property or personal advantage with intent to influence their vote, opinion, judgment, or official action; or the acceptance of such benefits by an official.
anchors.subcategory: Bribery of a Public Servant / Official

anchors.phrase: Theft by Virtue of Employment (Embezzlement Baseline)—By virtue of office, business, or employment, intentionally having possession or custody of movable property or funds of another, and intentionally converting or misallocating such property without the owner's consent, where the value does not exceed $2,500.

Theft by Virtue of Employment (Aggravated Felony Tiers)—Intentionally converting or misallocating funds or property held in active fiduciary custody by virtue of office or employment without consent, where the aggregate stolen value exceeds $2,500.

Perjury—Willfully making a false material statement under oath or affirmation in a matter, cause, action, or proceeding before a court, judge, magistrate, referee, or administrative body authorized to administer oaths.

Subornation of Perjury—Intentionally procuring, instigating, commanding, or inducing another person to commit perjury by giving false material testimony under oath in a judicial or administrative proceeding.

False Statements (Unsworn Falsification)—Making a false written statement which the actor does not believe to be true, on or in a form bearing notice authorized by law that false statements made therein are punishable, with intent to mislead a public servant in the performance of their duty.

Fraudulent Writings (Corporate Ledger Falsification)—Any person who, with intent to injure or defraud, falsifies, alters, or destroys any corporate books, records, accounts, financial statements, or balance sheets belonging to a business corporation or commercial enterprise.

Counterfeiting and Trademark Infringement—Intentionally manufacturing, counterfeiting, replicating, or uttering any registered trademark, commercial label, or financial designator of another without authorization and with intent to pass goods off as authentic.

Fraudulent Deceptive Marketing—Making, publishing, or circulating any statement, representation, or advertisement regarding real estate, insurance, merchandise, or services which contains any assertion, representation, or statement which is untrue, deceptive, or misleading with intent to induce public commerce.

Fraudulent Use of Financial Transaction Card—Using a financial transaction card or card security codes for the purpose of obtaining money, goods, or services with intent to defraud and knowing that the card is forged, expired, revoked, or used without the consent of the issuer or cardholder.

Computer Crimes Against Data or Programs—Intentionally accessing, modifying, destroying, copying, or taking possession of any computer, system, network, or data infrastructure without the consent of the owner and with intent to execute a fraud scheme or disrupt logistics.

Fraudulent Insurance Claims—Intentionally making or presenting any false or fraudulent statement or claim for payment of a loss under an insurance policy, or preparing records with intent to use them to support an unpermitted claim strategy.

Receiving Stolen Property—Intentionally receiving, buying, concealing, or facilitating the transfer of stolen movable property of another knowing or having reason to believe that the property was stolen.

Theft of Telecommunications or Public Utility Services—Intentionally obtaining or attempting to obtain telecommunications, electricity, gas, water, or corporate data infrastructure services without consent by use of a deceptive device, tampering manipulation, or fraudulent credentials.

Mail and Package Theft—Intentionally taking, concealing, or misdirecting mail matter or packages from a post office, common carrier truck, or private residential doorstep without the consent of the addressee or sender.

Attempted Embezzlement—Performing acts demonstrating unequivocally an intent to convert or misappropriate corporate, public, or bank assets held in a fiduciary capacity without consent, which are interrupted or fall short of actual asset transfer.

Attempted Public Bribery—Performing acts demonstrating unequivocally an intent to transfer, offer, or promise any property or advantage to a public servant to influence official action, which are caught, refused, or fall short of execution.
anchors.subcategory: Bribery of a Public Servant / Official

anchors.phrase: Arrest Record for Racketeering or Felony Embezzlement—The physical taking of a person into custody or formal booking on probable cause for state-level enterprise corruption (WOCCA), complex racketeering syndicates, or high-tier felony embezzlement, generating a permanent tracking entry prior to final court adjudication [CIB].

Arrest Record for Misdemeanor Fraud or Falsification—The physical taking of a person into custody or formal booking on probable cause for a misdemeanor-tier white-collar infraction, such as unsworn oral falsification or minor deceptive trade violations, logging a fingerprint trail prior to trial.

Warrant for Failure to Appear (Tax Fraud / White-Collar Nexus)—A judicially issued bench warrant commanding law enforcement to arrest an individual or corporate agent who failed to appear in court for an active criminal tax evasion, embezzlement, or corporate record falsification proceeding.

WOCCA Civil Restitution and Forfeiture Order—A non-criminal, post-investigation court injunction or final summary judgment ordering the complete dissolution, charter cancellation, or seizure of real property, corporate cash, and investments tied to a documented pattern of racketeering activity under Chapter 946.
Simple Battery—Intentionally causing bodily harm to another human being by an act done without the express consent of the person harmed.
Substantiating Bodily Harm (Substantial Battery)—Intentionally causing substantial bodily harm to another human being by an act done without the express consent of the person harmed.

Aggravated Battery (Great Bodily Harm)—Intentionally causing great bodily harm to another human being by an act done without the express consent of the person harmed, under circumstances that create a substantial risk of death, permanent disfigurement, or prolonged impairment.

Domestic Abuse Battery—An act of battery under § 940.19 committed by an adult against his or her spouse or former spouse, against an adult with whom the person resides or formerly resided, or against an adult with whom the person has a child in common.

Battery to an Emergency Medical Care Provider—Intentionally causing bodily harm to an emergency medical services practitioner, an emergency medical responder, or a person acting under the direction of an emergency medical services practitioner, without the consent of the person harmed, while the victim is acting in an official capacity.

Strangulation and Suffocation—Intentionally impeding the normal breathing or circulation of blood by applying pressure on the throat or neck or by blocking the nose or mouth of another person without consent.

First-Degree Reckless Endangering Safety—Recklessly endangering another person's safety under circumstances which show utter disregard for human life. (Wisconsin's structural equivalent to aggravated felonious assault when a weapon is discharged or an extreme life-threatening environment is manufactured without completed battery).

Second-Degree Reckless Endangering Safety—Recklessly endangering another person's safety. (Wisconsin's structural baseline for severe menacing, vehicular close-calls, or non-contact weapons handling that places an individual in immediate peril without explicit proof of utter disregard for life).

Threats to Injure or Accuse of Crime (Coercion)—Maliciously threatening to accuse another of any crime or offense, or threatening injury to the person, property, or business of another, with intent to compel the person so threatened to do any act against his or her will or omit to do any lawful act.

Intimidation of a Witness / Victim (Aggravated Weapon Tier)—Knowingly and maliciously preventing or discouraging, or attempting to prevent or discourage, a victim or witness from reporting a crime, cooperating with law enforcement, or testifying, accompanied by the deployment, usage, or threat of a dangerous weapon.

Bomb Scares (Terroristic Threatening baseline)—Intentionally conveying or causing to be conveyed any false information, knowing such information to be false, concerning an attempt or alleged attempt being made or to be made to destroy any property by means of explosives or to cause bodily harm/death via chemical, biological, or nuclear device tracking matrices.

Hostage Taking / Kidnapping for Ransom—Seizing or confining any person without consent, and holding them as a hostage or for ransom to compel another person or governmental body to perform or abstain from any act, or as a shield or warning.

Stalking (Baseline)—Engaging in a course of conduct directed at a specific person that would cause a reasonable person to suffer serious emotional distress or to fear bodily injury or death to himself or herself or a member of his or her immediate family, and the actor's acts actually cause such fear or distress.

Aggravated Stalking—Engaging in a course of conduct directed at a specific person that causes reasonable fear of bodily injury or death, enhanced by the usage or threat of a dangerous weapon, targeting a minor, violating a protective injunction, or carrying a prior conviction within a 7-year tracking window.

Criminal Harassment—Engaging in a course of conduct with intent to harass or intimidate another person, including repeatedly striking, shoving, kicking, or otherwise subjecting the person to physical contact, or repeatedly engaging in acts that masquerade as communications or tracking without a legitimate purpose.

Hazing (Institutional/Student Networks)—Forcing or requiring abusive physical or mental conditioning as an element of initiation or admission into any student organization, fraternity, sorority, or institutional network, which creates a substantial risk of physical health degradation or bodily harm.

Attempted Aggravated Battery—Performing actions demonstrating unequivocally an intent to inflict great bodily harm to another without consent, which are caught, prevented, or fall short of completed physical execution.

Attempted Kidnapping—Performing acts demonstrating unequivocally an intent to seize, confine, or carry away another person by force or threat without consent, which are interrupted, caught, or fall short of containment execution.

Domestic Abuse Injunction / Restraining Order—A judicially issued civil protective mandate restricting an individual from entering the shared residence, place of employment, or immediate physical proximity of a petitioner who has established reasonable grounds to fear domestic abuse.
anchors.subcategory: Civil Domestic Abuse Restraining Orders / Injunctions

anchors.phrase: Intimidating a Witness (Baseline)—Except as provided in § 940.43, knowingly and maliciously preventing or discouraging, or attempting to prevent or discourage, any witness from attending or giving testimony at any trial, proceeding, or authorized inquiry.

Felony Intimidation of a Witness—Intentionally preventing or discouraging, or attempting to prevent or discourage, any person who has been or may be a witness from giving testimony, where the act is accompanied by force, threats of injury, or part of a conspiracy.

Simulating Legal Process—Sending or delivering to another any document which simulates a summons, complaint, writ, subpoena, or other judicial process with intent to lead the recipient to believe it is genuine.

Obstructing an Officer via Falsification—Knowingly providing false information to an officer with intent to mislead them in an official investigation, or fabricating evidence to implicate an innocent person.

Impersonating a Law Enforcement Officer—Intentionally impersonating a peace officer, warden, or state trooper with intent to mislead others into believing the actor is a law enforcement professional, or performing any official act under such simulation.

Arbitrary Public Records Refusal—An official record custodian or public servant willfully, arbitrarily, or capriciously refusing or delaying access to a legally formatted public records request under the Wisconsin Open Records Law framework.

Misconduct in Public Office—A public officer or public employee intentionally performing an act which they know is in excess of their lawful authority, intentionally failing to perform a mandatory duty required by law, or exercising authority with a known corrupt motive for personal advantage.

Private Interest in Public Contract—A public officer or employee acting in their official capacity to negotiate, bid, or enter into a public contract in which they carry a private pecuniary interest or stock allocation exceeding standard nominal statutory caps.
Communicating with Jurors—Attempting to influence the decision of any juror in a civil or criminal case by communicating with them outside of official court protocols, whether orally or in writing.

Intentional Disclosure of Protected Health Data—Knowingly and willfully violating state confidentiality perimeters by disclosing, stealing, or using patient healthcare records or criminal justice information abstracts for personal benefit or commercial leverage.

Impersonating a Public Officer—Falsely assuming or pretending to be a public officer, a public employee, a peace officer, or an inspector, and acting as such or compelling others to submit to faux regulatory authority.

Compounding a Crime—Accepting or altering any financial consideration, property asset, or reward upon an agreement or understanding that the actor will conceal a felony crime, withhold evidence, or abstain from criminal prosecution.

Falsification of Financial Statement—Intentionally making a false or fraudulent material representation on an officially submitted Statement of Financial Interest required of state public officials, candidates, or designated fiduciaries.

Obstructing an Officer by False Sincerity—Knowingly delivering false information or placing an intentionally fabricated narrative line to a law enforcement officer with intent to mislead them in the performance of their official duties.

Unlawful Disclosure of CIB / TIME Data—An employee of a law enforcement agency intentionally accessing, utilizing, or disclosing confidential criminal history records, fingerprint files, or police database information for an unpermitted, non-law-enforcement purpose.

Punitive Contempt Adjudication—The intentional disobedience, resistance, or obstruction of the authority, process, or order of a court, prosecuted under criminal procedure rules to punish past non-compliance.
Resisting or Obstructing an Officer—Knowingly resisting or obstructing an officer while such officer is doing any act in an official capacity and with lawful authority.

Destroying or Altering Evidence (Hindering Apprehension)—Intentionally destroying, altering, hiding, or fabricating physical evidence, or placing false evidence, with intent to prevent the apprehension, prosecution, or conviction of a person who has committed a felony crime.

Harboring a Felon (Hindering Apprehension)—With intent to prevent the apprehension, prosecution, or conviction of a person who has committed a felony crime, knowingly harboring, lodging, or concealing that person.
Felony Bail Jumping—Intentionally violating any non-financial or financial condition of a bond or pre-trial release order executed during a pending felony charge or grand jury indictment.

Escape by a Convicted Felon—An individual sentenced or held in custody on a felony charge intentionally escaping or fleeing from a state prison, county jail, correctional facility, or the physical custody of a law enforcement officer.

Refusing to Aid Officer—Without a reasonable excuse, refusing or neglecting to render assistance to an officer when commanded to do so by an officer who has lawful authority to command such assistance in the execution of criminal arrest lines.
False Alarms—Intentionally giving or causing to be given a false alarm of fire or emergency medical need, or tampering with public safety communication lines or municipal signal boxes.

False Report of an Emergency—Intentionally conveying or causing to be conveyed to an emergency service provider false information concerning an emergency, or reporting a crime that did not happen to divert emergency personnel.

Fleeing or Eluding an Officer—Operating a motor vehicle on a highway and, after receiving a visual or audible signal from a traffic officer, knowingly fleeing or attempting to elude the officer by willful or wanton disregard of such signal or by increasing speed or extinguishing lights.

Treason Against the State / High Obstructive Subversion—Levying war against the state, adhering to its enemies, or giving them aid and comfort; or intentionally orchestrating the physical destruction of state government operation hubs to permanently seize administrative controls.

Misconduct in Public Office (Omission Tier)—A public officer or public employee intentionally failing or refusing to perform a mandatory, non-discretionary duty required of them by law, in their official capacity.

Misconduct in Office (Exercise of Unlawful Power)—A public officer or public employee intentionally performing an act which the officer knows is in excess of the officer's lawful authority, or which the officer knows is forbidden by law to be performed in that official capacity.

Misconduct in Office (Personal Benefit Tier)—A public officer or public employee intentionally falsifying profiles, overcharging fees, or exercising official authority to secure an unpermitted personal benefit, advantage, or financial gain for themselves or a third party.

Misconduct in Office (Financial Speculation Tier)—A public officer or public employee who, in an official capacity, buys or receives any claim, certificate, or public warrant at a discount, or speculates on state infrastructure assets with intent to secure personal financial gain from public liabilities.

Felony Lobbying Corrupt Acts—Any lobbyist, principal, or public officer who willfully falsifies a lobbying registry report, provides illegal financial consideration to a lawmaker to manipulate bill positioning, or falsifies required corporate expenditure statements under Chapter 13.

Altering Legislative Documents—Intentionally destroying, defacing, secreting, or making a false entry in any official sessional record, enrolled bill, or legislative act filed or deposited with the state or assembly clerks.

Telecommunications Service Theft / Simulating Infrastructure—Manufacturing, distributing, or utilizing electronic device modifications, network spoofing programs, or intercepted utility signals to scan, clone, or siphon telecommunications bandwidth without merchant consent.

Filing a Fraudulent Lien—Intentionally directing, organizing, or filing a false lien, mock financing statement (UCC-1), or property cloud encumbrance against the real or personal property of a public officer, judge, or public employee knowing the filing is a fabrication and intended to harass or retaliate.

Unauthorized Use of an Entity's or Individual's Identity—Intentionally using or attempting to use a financial, personal, or administrative identifier of another individual or corporate entity, dead or alive, to avoid a civil or criminal process, or to hinder law enforcement tracking loops.

Filing Fraudulent Liens—Intentionally presenting for filing or recording any lien, financing statement, or cloud on property title against a public official or public employee knowing that the document is groundless or fabricated.

Corporate Audit Record Deception—Intentionally concealing, altering, shredding, or destroying corporate records, bank ledgers, or trust documents while a state DFI or DOJ financial investigation is active or pending.

Refusal to Exhibit Driver License—The intentional failure or refusal of a motor vehicle operator to display or permit an officer to physically inspect their operator's license or state identity document upon a lawful traffic safety stop.

Simulating Court Operations—Organizing, convening, or participating in any unpermitted or mock judicial tribunal, common law court, or administrative panel intending to issue fake declarations or coercion orders against state citizens or property assets.

Unauthorized Disclosure of Justice Data—Accessing the state Transaction Information for Management of Enforcement (TIME) network or CIB databases to copy, leak, or disclose confidential criminal justice histories or tracking records for unpermitted or personal usage.

Obstructing an Officer via False Information—Knowingly giving false information, a fabricated identity, or false statements to an officer while that officer is acting in an official capacity with lawful authority, with intent to mislead the officer.

Unauthorized Practice of Law—Engaging in the practice of law, appearing in a court of record on behalf of another, or holding oneself out as an attorney authorized to practice law in Wisconsin without a valid active license from the State Bar.

Failure to Provide DNA Sample—An institutional prisoner, parolee, or felon on extended supervision intentionally refusing or failing to submit a biological sample (buccal swab or blood) requested by the department for mandatory integration into the state DOJ identification databank.

Simulating Emergency Safety Alerts—Interfering with, replicating, or transmitting a fraudulent civil defense alert, weather sirens network system signal, or municipal emergency evacuation broadcast with intent to trigger mass public panic or infrastructure gridlock.

Impersonating a Transit or Utility Worker—Falsely representing oneself to be an employee or authorized structural inspector of a public utility, telecommunications carrier, or cooperative association, with intent to secure unpermitted entry into private residences, grid networks, or secure facilities.

Injury to Transmission Lines / Sabotage—Intentionally cutting, damaging, disrupting, or misaligning any public utility line, fiber optic array, electrical substation component, or emergency communication tower infrastructure without consent.

False Emergency Reports—Intentionally dialing 911, activating an emergency duress node, or communicating an unverified emergency report to a public protection dispatch agency knowing that no emergency exists.

Harassment (Municipal Adoption)—Engaging in a course of conduct or repeatedly committing acts that intimidate or alarm another person, including non-physical digital messaging or tracking within city lines, which serve no legitimate purpose.
Unlawful Municipal Water Connection—Making an unpermitted connection or tapping into any public city water main or sewer lateral without authorization from the commissioner of public works.

Attempted Animal Fighting—Performing acts demonstrating unequivocally an intent to instigate, finance, or operate a dogfighting or animal-fighting exhibition, which are interrupted or prevented prior to execution.

Active Arrest Warrant (Felony Animal Cruelty Nexus)—A judicially authorized order commanding law enforcement to arrest a named individual based on a showing of probable cause for felony-tier animal torture, mutilation, or abandonment.

Active DNR Conservation Warrant—A judicially authorized arrest warrant commanding natural resource wardens or law enforcement to apprehend an individual for uncorrected big game poaching out of season or egregious environmental resource theft.

Arrest Booking for Animal Fighting Operations—The physical taking of a person into custody or formal booking on probable cause for felony-tier animal fighting, baiting enterprise, or dogfighting rings, generating a permanent tracking entry prior to final court adjudication.

Arrest Booking for Telecommunication Interception—The physical taking of a person into custody or formal booking on probable cause for felony electronic wiretapping, surveillance interception, or data-line bugging, generating a permanent tracking footprint prior to trial.

Warrant for Failure to Appear (Weights & Measures Nexus)—A judicially issued bench warrant commanding law enforcement to arrest an individual or corporate trader who failed to appear in court for an active criminal metric scaling fraud, device tampering, or agricultural consumer protection proceeding.

Warrant for Failure to Appear (DNR Conservation Nexus)—A judicially issued bench warrant commanding law enforcement or DNR wardens to apprehend an individual who failed to appear in court for an active criminal poaching, commercial wildlife trafficking, or biological resource violation.

Unlawful Lobbying Gifts—Any lobbyist or principal intentionally giving, offering, or promising anything of pecuniary value (including campaign contributions outside allowed window loops or illegal hospitality gifts) to an elected state official, legislative employee, or candidate for state office.
anchors.subcategory: Bribery of a Public Servant / Official

anchors.phrase: Act of Terrorism—Intentionally committing a felony act causing mass bodily harm, death, or catastrophic infrastructure damage, with the explicit intent to intimidate or coerce a civilian population or influence the policy of a government unit by intimidation or coercion.

Fraudulent Academic Credentials—Operating an unaccredited post-secondary institution and falsely representing it as accredited, or conferring fake academic degrees, diplomas, or credentials for financial compensation.

Habitual Truancy (Parental Failure)—A parent or guardian failing to compel a child's school attendance after receiving formal statutory notice that the child qualifies as a habitual truant (absent from school without an acceptable excuse for part or all of 5 or more days on which fame is held during a school semester).

Operating Aircraft Under Influence—Operating an aircraft in the air, on the ground, or on the water while under the influence of an intoxicant or a restricted controlled substance, or in a careless or reckless manner so as to endanger the life or property of another.

Operating While Intoxicated (1st Offense)—Operating a motor vehicle on a public highway while under the influence of an intoxicant, a controlled substance, or a combination thereof, to a degree which renders the person incapable of safely driving; or driving with a blood alcohol concentration (BAC) of 0.08% or more.
Operating While Intoxicated (2nd Offense)—Operating a motor vehicle on a public highway within 10 years of a prior OWI conviction, suspension, or implied consent refusal.

Commercial Driver License OWI—Driving or operating a commercial motor vehicle (CMV) on a public highway with a blood alcohol concentration (BAC) of 0.04% or more, or while under the influence of an intoxicant.

Inattentive Driving / Handheld Device Use—Operating a motor vehicle on a public highway while using a handheld wireless telephone or text-messaging device, or engaging in any conduct that interferes with the safe operation of the vehicle.

Texting in School/Construction Zone—Operating a motor vehicle on a public highway and using a handheld wireless device to compose, send, or read an electronic message while driving through a posted school zone, school crossing, or active highway maintenance construction area.
Reckless Driving—Endangering the safety of any person or property by the negligent operation of a vehicle on a public highway or on any premises open to the public for vehicular traffic.

Speed Unreasonable and Imprudent (Careless Driving)—Operating a motor vehicle on a public highway at a speed greater than is reasonable and prudent under the conditions and having regard for the actual and potential hazards then existing.

Overtaking or Passing Stopped School Bus—The operator of a vehicle overtaking or meeting from the front or rear any school bus which has stopped on a highway and is displaying flashing red warning lights, failing to stop the vehicle not less than 20 feet from the bus and remain stopped until the bus resumes motion or the flashing lights are extinguished.
Speeding (General Moving Infraction)—Operating a motor vehicle on a public highway in excess of a statutorily established or posted speed limit.

Hit and Run (Attended Vehicle Property Damage)—Failing to immediately stop a vehicle at the scene of an accident involving damage to an attended vehicle, or failing to remain at the scene to provide identity, operator license, and registration details to the other driver.
anchors.subcategory: Hit and Run — Property Damage Only (Misdemeanor)

anchors.phrase: Felony Hit and Run—The operator of a vehicle involved in an accident resulting in injury or death to any person, failing to immediately stop, remain at the scene to provide identifying data, and render reasonable assistance to the injured person.
anchors.subcategory: Hit and Run — Property Damage Only (Misdemeanor)

anchors.phrase: Operating After Suspension (OWS)—Operating a motor vehicle on a public highway during any period in which the person's operating privilege is suspended by the state or an administrative order.

Operating Unregistered Vehicle—Operating or permitting the operation of a motor vehicle on a public highway that is not registered with WisDOT or for which the registration has been suspended, revoked, or expired.

Commercial Driver License Disqualification—An administrative declaration by WisDOT forcing the long-term or permanent removal of an individual's commercial motor vehicle (CMV) operating authority following the accumulation of major traffic offenses, railroad crossing violations, or serious safety failures under FMCSR guidelines.

Operating While Revoked (OWI Nexus)—Operating a motor vehicle on a public highway during a period in which the person's operating privilege is revoked by WisDOT or a criminal court judge following a prior major conviction (such as an OWI, felony fleeing, or fatal hit and run).

Hit and Run (Property Damage Only)—Failing to immediately stop a vehicle after an accident on a highway or public premises involving damage to an attended vehicle, or failing to remain at the scene to fulfill statutory identity and insurance data exchange requirements.
anchors.subcategory: Hit and Run — Property Damage Only (Misdemeanor)

anchors.phrase: Hit and Run Involving Injury or Death—Failing to immediately stop a vehicle after an accident on a highway or public premises resulting in injury or death to any person, or failing to remain at the scene to provide mandatory reasonable assistance and statutory identity data exchange.
anchors.subcategory: Hit and Run — Property Damage Only (Misdemeanor)

anchors.phrase: Vehicle Registration Suspension—An administrative order issued by WisDOT suspending a vehicle's registration or title plates following an owner's failure to maintain mandatory liability insurance, non-payment of traffic fines, or refusal to comply with emissions testing.

Hit and Run (Aggravated Death/Injury Tier)—An operator of a vehicle involved in an accident resulting in great bodily harm or death to any person failing to immediately stop their vehicle at the scene, fulfill mandatory identification exchanges, and render reasonable medical assistance.
anchors.subcategory: Hit and Run — Property Damage Only (Misdemeanor)

anchors.phrase: Operating While Revoked (Criminal Tier)—Operating a motor vehicle on a public highway knowing that one's operating privilege has been suspended or revoked, where the underlying revocation was caused by an OWI, chemical test refusal, or vehicular homicide tracking index.

Operating Vehicle with Suspended/Altered Registration—Driving or permitting the operation of a motor vehicle on a public highway when the vehicle's state registration is suspended, revoked, or canceled, or displaying a simulated, altered, or unassigned license plate.

First-Degree Sexual Assault—Intentionally engaging in sexual intercourse or sexual contact with another person without consent, where the act causes great bodily harm, or is executed by use or threat of a dangerous weapon, or occurs with a non-consenting person aided by group force/co-actors.

Second-Degree Sexual Assault—Intentionally engaging in sexual intercourse or sexual contact with another person without consent by use or threat of force or violence, or with a person the actor knows is mentally incapacitated, physically helpless, or unconscious.

Third-Degree Sexual Assault—Intentionally engaging in sexual intercourse with another person without that person's consent, absent aggravating weapon, injury, or force factors required under first or second degree frameworks.

Fourth-Degree Sexual Assault—Intentionally engaging in sexual contact with another person without that person's consent (covers unpermitted physical touching of intimate areas for sexual gratification).
First-Degree Sexual Assault of a Child—Intentionally engaging in sexual intercourse or sexual contact with a child who has not attained the age of 12 years.

Second-Degree Sexual Assault of a Child—Intentionally engaging in sexual intercourse or sexual contact with another person who has attained the age of 12 years but has not attained the age of 16 years.

Repeated Sexual Assault of Same Child—Engaging in 3 or more violations of child sexual assault under § 948.02 against the same child within a specified tracking window, combined into a single continuous count index.

Possession of Child Pornography—Knowingly possessing, viewing, reproducing, downloading, or storing any photograph, film, videotape, print, or digital image depicting a child engaging in sexually explicit conduct.

Production of Explicit Child Material—Knowingly employing, using, persuading, enticing, or coercing a child under 18 years of age to engage in sexually explicit conduct for the purpose of photographing, filming, or recording.

Computer Enticement of a Child—Using a computer system, mobile telephone network, or digital electronic communication service to communicate with a child under 18 years of age with intent to induce or entice the child to engage in illegal sexual activity.

Public Sexual Indecency—Intentionally engaging in an open act of sexual intercourse or sexual contact in a public place under circumstances in which the actor knows their conduct will be openly observed; or indecently exposing a sex organ in a public setting.

Sexual Exposure to a Minor—Intentionally exposing one's genitals, pubic area, or anus to a child under the age of 16 years, or causing a child under 16 to expose their own intimate areas, for purposes of sexual gratification or arousal of the actor.

Professional Sexual Exploitation—Any person who is or represents themselves to be a therapist, physician, or professional counselor intentionally having sexual contact or sexual intercourse with a patient or client during, or under the pretext of, ongoing professional treatment or consultation.

Capture of Intimate Representation—Intentionally capturing an intimate representation of another person (via hidden cameras, smartphone recording tools, or electronic bugs) without the person's consent, under circumstances where the person has a reasonable expectation of privacy.

Soliciting a Child for Prostitution—Intentionally soliciting, encouraging, or enticing any child under the age of 18 years to practice prostitution, or receiving anything of value from the commercial sexual exploitation of a child.

Exposing Child to Harmful Matter—Intentionally exhibiting, selling, distributing, or displaying any harmful explicit material (photographs, printed media, or digital representations showing nudity or explicit conduct) to a child under the age of 18 years.

Institutional Custodial Sexual Assault—Any correctional employee, state agent, or facility contractor intentionally engaging in sexual intercourse or sexual contact with an inmate or resident confined in a staff-monitored prison, jail, workhouse, or secure care center.

Failure to Maintain Sex Offender Registration—Knowingly failing to comply with mandatory sex offender registration updates, failing to verify a changing residential address, employment location, or school enrollment ledger within required statutory windows.

Fourth-Degree Sexual Assault of a Child—Intentionally engaging in sexual contact (intentional touching of intimate parts or clothing covering them for sexual gratification) with a child under the age of 16 years, without consent, absent the use of force, threat, or weapons.

Soliciting a Child for Prostitution—Intentionally soliciting, encouraging, or enticing any child under the age of 18 years to practice prostitution, or slanting a transactional payment asset to secure a commercial sex act with a minor.

Exposing Minor to Harmful Material—Intentionally exhibiting, showing, or displaying harmful or explicit obscene material (including digital prints, photographs, or simulations depicting explicit nudity or ultimate sexual conduct) to a child under the age of 18 years, or selling/loaning such material to a child for commercial profit.

Arrest Booking for Felony Sexual Assault—The physical taking into custody, fingerprinting, and photographic logging of an individual based on probable cause for felony sexual assault under § 940.225, creating an un-adjudicated tracking footprint.

Arrest Booking for Child Sex Trafficking—The physical arrest and booking of an individual based on probable cause for trafficking minors for commercial sexual servitude under § 948.051, creating a pre-trial tracking footprint.

Warrant for Failure to Appear (Registry Nexus)—A judicially issued bench warrant commanding law enforcement to arrest a registered sex offender who failed to appear in court for an active felony registry non-compliance, address update failure, or evasion proceeding under § 301.45.
anchors.subcategory: Active Sex Offender Registry Fugitive Bench Warrants

anchors.phrase: Unauthorized Recording of Intimate Areas—Capturing an intimate representation of another person without their consent, in a place where the person has a reasonable expectation of privacy, or distributing such a representation knowing it was captured without consent.

Arrest Booking for Felony Sexual Assault (Alternate Citation)—The physical taking of a person into custody or formal booking on probable cause for felony sexual assault or forcible rape, generating a permanent tracking fingerprint trail and immediate state Crime Information Bureau (CIB) database entries prior to trial.

Arrest Booking for Human Trafficking—The physical booking and taking into custody on probable cause for felony sex trafficking, human trafficking, or commercial sexual servitude, establishing immediate tracking loops prior to formal trial.

Warrant for Failure to Appear (DNA Databank Nexus)—A judicially issued bench warrant commanding law enforcement to arrest an individual who failed to appear in court or report to a testing facility for a mandatory pre-trial DNA specimen collection order issued under state felony sex offense protocols.

Attempted Child Pornography Production—Performing acts demonstrating unequivocally an intent to employ, use, persuade, entice, or coerce a child under 18 years of age to engage in sexually explicit conduct for the purpose of photographing, filming, or recording, which are prevented or cut off prior to successful capture.

Public Lewdness or Window Peeping (Municipal)—Loitering or crouching in a public or private space for the purpose of looking, peeping, or peering into the windows, doors, or private residential openings of another without consent, or engaging in minor lewd gestures below state criminal code thresholds.

Unlawful Hazardous Waste Disposal—Intentionally transporting, storing, treating, or disposing of any hazardous waste compound without a valid DNR license, or making false statements in mandatory toxic manifest ledgers.

Commercial Food Adulteration—Manufacturing, packaging, holding, or offering for sale any adulterated, contaminated, or filthy food product intended for human consumption within state commerce pipelines.

Public Assistance Provider Fraud—Knowingly making false statements or concealing material facts to claim unpermitted payments under a medical assistance program, or offering/accepting illegal kickbacks or rebates to alter referral tracks.

Active Environmental Felony Warrant—An open, judicially executed arrest warrant commanding law enforcement officers and DNR wardens to locate and physically apprehend an individual or corporate agent indicted for high-tier toxic dumping, hazardous waste storage violations, or environmental record falsification.

Attempted Healthcare Fraud—Performing acts demonstrating unequivocally an intent to submit fraudulent billing ledgers or execute an illegal kickback scheme under a medical assistance program, which are detected, intercepted, or fall short of completed transaction draw.

Fraudulent Wage Non-Payment—An employer intentionally, and with intent to defraud, failing, refusing, or omitting to pay a worker all accrued wages or earned compensation due, where the unpaid asset balance crosses felony limits.

Unlawful Employment of Minors—An employer or parent permitting, hiring, or forcing a child under 18 years of age to work in a hazardous, prohibited, or sexually explicit commercial trade, or violating statutory hour limits post-warning.
